data.title: Warren Buffett's 38 Dog Berkshire February Report
data.description:
Foreword

Dan Burrows says in Kiplinger Investing:

"The Berkshire Hathaway portfolio is a diverse set of blue chips and, increasingly, lesser-known growth bets. Here's a look at every stock picked by Warren Buffett and his lieutenants.

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) added just one stock to its holdings in Q4: Constellation Brands (STZ), the beer and wine and spirit maker whose brands include Corona Extra, Modelo Especial and Robert Mondavi Winery. BRK.B bought 5.6 million shares worth $1.2 billion as of the end of Q4. At less than 0.5% of the portfolio, STZ is Berkshire's 19th largest holding.

It should come as little surprise that Berkshire continued to add to some favored holdings. Buffett boosted the conglomerate's investment in Occidental Petroleum (OXY) by 3.5%, or 8.9 million shares in Q4. With 265 million shares worth $12.4 billion, OXY is Berkshire's sixth most important position. Note that OXY shares lost almost a fifth of their value last year and traded as low as $46 at one point in December. It wouldn't be the first time Buffett went bargain hunting in OXY stock, which he first bought for Berkshire in 2022.

Elsewhere, Berkshire added to recent additions, such as Domino's Pizza (DPZ) and Pool (POOL). The holding company initiated the stakes in the third quarter of 2024 and the size suggests they were not Buffett's picks. If you want to know which stocks Warren Buffett is buying and selling, look no further than the Berkshire Hathaway's holdings."

Any collection of stocks is more clearly understood when subjected to yield-based (dogcatcher) analysis, this Buffett/Berkshire batch is perfect for the dogcatcher process. Here are the December 31 data for 26 dividend paying stocks in the Kiplinger and YCharts-documented collection of 38 now owned by Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway firm as of 2/19/25. Updated quarterly per BRK SEC filings, the next report is due in Mid-May.

A rapid market recovery, following the Ides of March 2020 plunge, made the possibility of owning productive dividend shares from the Buffett collection out of reach for first-time investors.

This February 2025 update shows that the following two of the top ten dogs of Berkshire stocks by yield now live up to the dogcatcher ideal of paying annual dividends (from a $1K investment) exceeding their single share prices:

The Kraft Heinz Co. (KHC); Sirius XM (SIRI).

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced February Berkshire Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the nil "safer" ideal-priced choices in the Afterword, at the tail of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 12.55% To 42.74% Net Gains From Top-Ten Berkshire Dogs By February 2026

Seven of ten top Berkshire Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this February 2025 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 70% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding Berkshire stocks and the median of one-year target prices from analysts, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to January 2026 were:

Constellation Brands (the newest Berkshire acquisition) was projected to net $427.42, based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% less than the market as a whole.

Lennar (LEN) was projected to net $275.76, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 14 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 67% more than the market as a whole.

Jefferies Financial (JEF) was projected to net $269.32 based on the median of target prices estimated by 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 24% greater than the market as a whole.

Diageo (DEO) was projected to net $210.09, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 34% less than the market as a whole.

Ally Financial (ALLY) was projected to net $174.14, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 16 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 45% greater than the market as a whole.

Heico Corp (HEI) was projected to net $160.42, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 16 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 28% greater than the market as a whole.

Chevron (CVX) was projected to net $157.97, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 11% over the market as a whole.

Bank of America (BAC) was projected to net $155.83, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% over the market as a whole.

Kraft Heinz was projected to net $151.46, based on the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 53% less than the market as a whole.

Chubb (CB) was projected to net $125.49 based on the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% under the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 21.08% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten Berkshire stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 8% greater than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

38 Berkshire Holdings By Analyst Target Gain Estimates

The February 2025 Berkshire 38 By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top February Berkshire Stocks By Yield Ranged 2.26% To 5.37% Per YCharts

Top ten Berkshire Dogs occupied four of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Four consumer staple stalwarts, placed first, fifth, sixth and eighth, Kraft Heinz [1], Diageo [5], Coca-Cola (KO) [6], and Constellation Brands [8].

Then, in second place, was the lone energy dog, Chevron Corp [2]. A lone communication services dog placed third Sirius XM Holdings [3].

Finally, four financial stocks placed fourth, seventh, ninth, and tenth, Ally Financial [4], Citigroup (C) [7], Jefferies Financial [9], and Bank of America [10], to complete the February Dogs of Berkshire by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The pie chart above shows the relative strengths of the top ten February Berkshire dogs by dividend and price as of market close 2/19/2025.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by just two of the highest yield Dogs of BRK.

Kraft Heinz and Sirius XM Holdings both lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices.

However, eight more, Chevron, Ally Financial, Diageo, Coca-Cola, Citigroup, Constellation Brands, Jefferies, and Bank of America, are within striking distance and show prices under $50 per dividend dollar.

26 Berkshire Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 2/19/25

The lower chart reveals all 26 Berkshire-held Dividend dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the February Berkshire batch was Kraft Heinz at $18.62 per dividend dollar and the most expensive was Heico at $1010.10 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Four of Twenty-Six BRK Dividend Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Four Berkshire stocks so infected are Coca-Cola, Citigroup, Jefferies Financial, and Bank of America. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, and Tariffs, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Ten Berkshire Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, two in the top ten are already ideally priced. Besides those two, already in the ideal zone, six more top-yield stocks are within $70.07 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 10.78% To 41.40% Top Ten Berkshire Upsides To February 2026; (32) Three -0.65% to -4.37% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 13.67% Disadvantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Berkshire Dogs As of February 19, 2026

Ten-top Berkshire dogs were culled by yield for this monthly update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten Berkshire dogs selected 2/19/25 by yield, represented four of eleven sectors. Consumer Discretionary, Health Care, Industrials, Materials, Real Estate, Technology, and Utilities missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield Berkshire Dogs (34) To Deliver 14.8% Vs. (35) 17.14% Net Gains by All Ten Come February 19, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Berkshire Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 1.17% LESS gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The second lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Kraft Heinz, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 27.62%.

The five lowest-priced Berkshire top-yield dogs for February were: Sirius XM; Kraft Heinz; Ally; Bank of America; Jefferies, with prices ranging from $25.20 to $69.49.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of December 31 were: Coca-Cola; Citigroup; Diageo; Chevron. Constellation Brands, with prices ranging from $70.07 to $172.42.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria in the market fades, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements will gain momentum into 2025 with the exception of the few tech stocks, look for at least half of the ten BRK Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly.

Afterword

Buy No 'Safer' Berkshire February DiviDogs; Watch Six

Mid-February 2025,15 of the 38 BRK were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 2/19/25 data showed none were fair-priced.

How BRK 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price" Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that no dividends from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend BRK dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or less than) the price of a single share.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called "fair price."

A market correction of 88.2% is required to put "safer" survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There is, of course, another way for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends to the dogcatcher ideal level. That's a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much "good" elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Berkshire Updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnote

BRK Holdings Per SEC Form 13F as of 12/31/24

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Berkshire dividend dog stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from YCharts; Yahoo Finance - Stock Market Live, Quotes, Business & Finance News; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open-source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com

data.title: Rithm Capital: This Ultra-Safe 8% Yield Is Still On Sale
data.description:
In my last piece on Rithm Capital Corp. (NYSE:RITM) I pointed to the mortgage real estate investment trust’s acquisition-driven earnings growth as well as very high margin of safety as far as the dividend was concerned as buying points for the stock.

Rithm Capital still offers passive income investors a healthy 8% dividend yield, which was well-covered by the trust’s distributable profits in 4Q24.

Rithm Capital’s portfolio remained tilted toward Mortgage Servicing Rights, which are rate-dependent mortgage instruments that are poised to become more valuable in a rising-rate environment. With inflation re-accelerating since October, for four straight quarters, there is a strong argument to be made for Rithm Capital’s Mortgage Servicing Rights to profit from higher valuation multiples.

Moreover, Rithm Capital’s book value per share is growing and the mortgage trust paid out less than half of its distributable profits in the fourth quarter.

Strong Mortgage Trust With MSR-Tilted Investment Portfolio, Benefiting From Higher-For-Longer Rate Environment

Rithm Capital is a leading mortgage real estate investment trust with an investment focus on Mortgage Servicing Rights, Residential Mortgage Loans, Servicing Assets, Single-Family Rental Properties and, more recently, Asset Management.

Rithm Capital’s total balance sheet sum, as of December 31, 2024 has grown to exceed $45 billion, up 28% YoY thanks to the mortgage REIT’s acquisition of the Sculptor asset management platform in 4Q23.

Mortgage Servicing Rights are still the trust’s biggest block of assets, accounting for $10.3 billion of all investments, or 23% of all balance sheet assets. The second-biggest investment block were government-(backed) securities worth $9.7 billion.

Mortgage Servicing Rights are compelling investments for mortgage real estate investment trusts due to the directional nature of the price-interest rate relationship of these securities. Put simply, Mortgage Servicing Rights become more valuable for mortgage REITs during a rising-rate period whereas mortgage-backed securities, those that traditional mREITs like Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY) invest in, are more attractive mortgage investments when rates fall.

What benefits Rithm Capital here in more concrete terms is that inflation has re-accelerated in the last four months, with inflation moving back up to 3.0% in January 2025, the highest level since June 2024. Higher inflation means higher-for-longer interest rates, which is poised to profit Rithm Capital’s big, rate-dependent Mortgage Servicing Rights portfolio.

Valuation trends for Mortgage Servicing Rights are favorable and underscore the argument that I just made about MSRs being particularly attractive investments during periods of rising rates. In the fourth quarter, Mortgage Servicing Rights have managed to expand their multiples, which is a reflection of the fact that higher-for-longer interest rates fundamentally benefit holders of MSRs.

Not surprisingly, Mortgage Servicing Rights, and their servicing revenue, accounted for a fairly big slice of Rithm Capital’s total sales. Two segments are standing out here for the mortgage trust;

  1. Servicing and Asset Management. Servicing revenue, which relates to MSRs, was $1.83 billion in 2024, up 41% YoY; and
  2. Asset Management, which raked in total sales of $520 million, up 529% YoY.

Mortgage Servicing Rights profit from an extension of the underlying fee stream, while Asset Management is a fairly new business that Rithm Capital committed to in order to diversify its asset portfolio.

Rithm Capital’s Pay-Out Metrics Continue To Look Very Healthy

The main reason for owning the stock of mortgage real estate investment trust Rithm Capital relates to the fact that the trust has a very high margin of safety as far as the dividend is concerned. The mortgage trust earned $0.60 per share in distributable profits in 4Q24, +18% YoY, and the highest amount earned in the last year.

Rithm Capital’s distributable profits equate to a dividend pay-out ratio of 42%, less than half of the trust’s income. Rithm Capital obviously profits here from a strong performance in its main businesses, but particularly in Servicing and Asset Management.

5% Discount To BV

As I have demonstrated in the dividend pay-out chart above, Rithm Capital has a positive book value growth trend. As of December 31, 2024, the mortgage trust had a book value of $12.56, reflecting 6% YoY growth. This book value growth comes in addition to Rithm Capital’s 8% dividend yield that the stock presently pays.

In addition to this yield, passive income investors have the chance to gobble up shares below book value which, I think, is a great opportunity, particularly because mortgage-backed security REITs like Annaly Capital Management or AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) sell at premiums to book value of 13% and 16% respectively.

Taking into account that we are dealing with a higher-for-longer rate environment, I think that Rithm Capital offers more upside for investors as well. Thus, I think that Rithm Capital could re-rate to a 1.11-1.15x BV premium (equal to Annaly Capital Management’s and AGNC Investment’s valuation range), as long as the trust makes money on its MSRs and interest rates stay high. The implied intrinsic value here is $14.00-14.50 per share.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Disappoint Investors

Passive income investors should pay attention to inflation rates, as they will most certainly determine the interest rate path that the central bank will set. Higher inflation means a higher-for-longer rate environment, which is poised to drive the decompression of MSR valuation multiples.

On the flip side, a quick take-down of inflation and interest rates implies valuation headwinds and thus a diminished opportunity for Rithm Capital to profit from a stock re-rating.

My Conclusion

I have said in this piece, as I did in previous articles on the mortgage real estate investment trust, that Rithm Capital is primarily attractive for passive income investors for its very high margin of safety as far as the dividend is concerned.

Rithm Capital offers passive income investors an 8% yield and a 42% dividend pay-out ratio, which is kind of unheard of in the high-yield sector.

Rithm Capital should not be selling for less than book value, in my view, and with the trust continuing to grow its book value per share, I think the trust remains a top contender for an upside re-rating in 2025, particularly with reference to its Mortgage Servicing Rights and re-accelerating inflation. Strong Buy.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: A Great Opportunity Relative To Other Similar Firms
data.description:
To be honest with you, I don't know if I will ever get tired of the midstream/pipeline industry. Ever since my first year in college when I took a class called Energy and Society, I have found the energy space fascinating. But there is something especially interesting about this industry. Even though it is capital intensive to grow, the space generates steady and consistent cash flows. As the population grows, at least for now, so too should the potential for these businesses. And even though many investors love these types of companies for the hefty distributions they pay out, I personally just care about the fact that they are low risk and attractively priced.

One of the players that I like most in this space is Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD). At present, the company owns over 50,000 miles of pipelines. It has over 300 million barrels of storage capacity for things like crude oil, petrochemicals, refined products, and NGLs. Plus, it boasts 14 billion cubic feet of natural gas storage capacity. Since I last reaffirmed the company as a ‘buy’ candidate back in November 2024, the stock has achieved upside for investors of 6.9%. That's comfortably above the 3.3% upside achieved by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. But with the company continuing to grow, and shares attractively priced, I believe that further upside is appropriate. Because of this, I believe that reaffirming it as a ‘buy’ candidate is logical.

A necessary disclosure

As of the time of this writing, areas some data from the most recent quarter that management has not yet revealed. For instance, they haven't shown cash, so I cannot adequately calculate net debt on my own. However, they did provide the net leverage ratio of the company, which gives me the ability to approximate what overall net debt is. Another unknown would be the value of non-controlling interests. For the purpose of valuing the firm, I ended up using the non-controlling interest figure reported for the third quarter. Considering that this amounts to only 0.8% of the company’s estimated enterprise value, it is a rounding error. But I did think it was appropriate to at least disclose these small uncertainties.

An Update On Enterprise Products Partners

Considering that the last time I analyzed Enterprise Products Partners was back in November, we only have data covering for one additional quarter, which would be the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. For that time, revenue for the business came in at $14.20 billion. This is actually down from the $14.62 billion generated the previous year. To be honest, because of how this industry operates, I really don't think that revenue is all that important a metric. I also don't think that net profits are terribly important either. But for context, those did increase from $1.57 billion to $1.62 billion.

What's more important, in my view, is cash flow. And with only one exception, cash flows for the quarter came in higher than they were the previous year. This was driven almost entirely by the firm’s NGL segment. Higher processing volumes and higher average processing margins contributed $70 million of upside for the gross operating margin of this segment. The company also enjoyed higher sales volumes and higher average sales margins associated with its NGL marketing activities. That added another $66 million to profitability. There were other contributors as well, but none of those were terribly significant on their own. But because of these changes, segment margin for the company grew from $1.38 billion to $1.55 billion.

There was only one segment for the company, other than the NGL segment, that experienced any sort of profitability improvement year over year. That was the Natural Gas segment, which saw growth of $37 million from $286 million of profit to $323 million. $21 million of this increase came from greater gathering volumes and higher treating revenue associated with its Permian Basin gathering systems. Greater transportation and other related revenue helped to push its profits associated with its Texas intrastate system up by $19 million. And higher sales volumes pushed profits up for its natural gas marketing activities by $7 million.

Other parts of the company came in worse year over year. The Crude Oil segment reported a $39 million decline in profits because of lower sales volumes and a rise in operating expenses on a year-over-year basis. The company's Petrochemicals & Refined Products segment solve its margin drop $91 million. $46 million of this decline came from higher operating expense and a reduction in average sales margins associated with its propylene production activities. $30 million of this downside can be attributed to lower average sales margins associated with its octane enhancement facilities and related assets. The firm also suffered from a $24 million decline involving its refined products pipelines thanks to lower average sales margins.

Thanks to the overall increase that the company enjoyed, most cash flow metrics improved year over year. The one exception to this was operating cash flow, which contracted from $2.37 billion to $2.36 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital and non-controlling interests, we do get an increase from just under $2.20 billion to $2.34 billion. Whenever I look at Enterprise Products Partners, I also like to look at what I refer to as ‘true free cash flow’. This is essentially adjusted operating cash flow, minus sustaining capital expenditures.

Also known as maintenance capital expenditures, these are capital expenditures that are needed in order for the company to continue operating without focusing on any growth prospects. This doesn't punish the firm for its growth initiatives. Year over year, this metric expanded from $2.07 billion to $2.22 billion. DCF, or distributable cash flow, expanded from $2.06 billion to $2.16 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $2.50 billion to $2.60 billion.

In the chart above, you can also see financial performance for 2024 compared to 2023. Without exception, the picture improved from one year to the next. This was made possible in large part by major investments that the company had been making. During 2024 alone, management spent $5.5 billion on capital investments. $3.9 billion of this was dedicated toward growth projects. Another $949 million was dedicated to the firm's acquisition of Pinon Midstream, leaving the remaining $667 million for sustaining capital expenditures.

This is part of a trend for the company. The fact of the matter is that, through major investments, the company continues to expand. In the image above, for instance, you can see how volumes across parts of its network have changed in recent years. And the great thing is that this kind of expansion is set to continue. For 2025, management intends to allocate between $4 billion and $4.5 billion toward growth projects.

This is on top of $525 million of sustaining capital spending. And in 2026, the company is currently planning between $2 billion and $2.5 billion of growth-oriented expenditures. However, the sheer magnitude of what the company is doing cannot be adequately expressed just by looking at current growth plans. This is because some of its projects are multiyear in nature. In fact, for this year alone, management believes that around $6 billion worth of projects will come online. In all likelihood, this time next year, we will be talking about even more growth prospects than what is currently planned.

If we use the data currently available for 2024 and 2023, we can see in the chart above that Enterprise Products Partners is cheap enough that it doesn't even need to grow in order to justify upside. The stock is certainly in the mid to high range of what I would expect of a value play. However, in this industry, because of the consistency, safety, and robustness of cash flow margins, I believe that additional wiggle room is appropriate. In the table below, I used two of the valuation metrics to stack Enterprise Products Partners up against five similar firms. What I found was that only one of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is. That is none other than Energy Transfer (ET), which is my personal favorite in the space and a company that I own shares of.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Enterprise Products Partners 8.3 10.3
TC Energy (TRP) 9.0 12.1
Kinder Morgan (KMI) 9.4 15.2
Enbridge (ENB) 10.5 13.2
The Williams Companies (WMB) 14.1 15.1
Energy Transfer 6.5 9.9

There are some other things that we should take into consideration as well. For starters, there's the topic of leverage. Any sort of company that pays out hefty distributions and that benefits from significant capital spending is susceptible to overextending itself. Fortunately, Enterprise Products Partners might be amongst the healthiest prospects out there in this regard. Despite the major investments that the company has made in recent years, the net leverage ratio that the business has stands at only 3.10.

In the chart above, you can see that this is comfortably lower than any of the five companies I compared it to. In fact, if Enterprise Products Partners wanted to increase its net leverage ratio to the lowest of these five comparable firms, it would need to increase its net debt by $11.38 billion. This certainly affords the company a good amount of wiggle room. Furthermore, it justifies greater upside potential since it means that the overall risk profile for shareholders is more attractive.

Another thing that we should be looking at is the topic of yield. This is also an area that Enterprise Products Partners excels in. As the chart above illustrates, the overall yield of the company is currently 6.50%. This is higher than all but one of the five firms that I stacked it up against. Whenever I do this type of analysis, I also like to see what the payout ratio of these businesses is and then I like to normalize the yield to see what the picture would look like in the event that all of the companies paid out the same percentage of their operating cash flow toward shareholders. In the chart below, you can see what I mean. In this case, the disparity between Enterprise Products Partners and Energy Transfer worsens in a negative way. But other than that, the company still retains its position as the second highest yielding of the six companies. So not only do we have a firm that is cheap relative to most of the competition, we also have a firm that has a hefty payout and that is doing so even after looking at adjusted figures.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I have to say that I remain a fan of Enterprise Products Partners. Even though it's not my top choice in the space, it is certainly deserving of a bullish assessment. Shares are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to similar firms. The payout of the company is appealing and leverage is low. Add on top of all of this additional growth initiatives that management is focused on, and I believe it would be a mistake not to reaffirm the company as a solid ‘buy’ candidate.

data.title: Diamondback Energy: CEO Steps Down And Another Acquisition
data.description:
Diamondback Energy (NASDAQ:FANG) announced that the CEO, Travis Stice will step down (but remain on the board of directors as Chairman), and that the current President, Kaes Van’t Hof, will assume the CEO role. This overshadows an acquisition announcement and is probably more significant than the coming earnings report. Mr. Stice has been the only CEO since the company went public and has overseen some tremendous growth in that time period. He is leaving some very big shoes to fill. It is often far harder to replace a top performing CEO than it is to replace someone that is average because top performing executives are a small subset of the available executives for the position. Nonetheless, the company announced that succession planning was part of the announcement. That gives this succession a better chance than some other companies I follow.

Many times, the single most important asset is management. Management is an asset that is not on the balance sheet. But evaluation of management by an investor may well be more important than anything on the balance sheet or anywhere else in the corporation. Management is often the key to an investment success.

At the same time, the new CEO should be given some time before any final evaluation is made. There will likely be a transition period where the new company will have to get used to the new CEO. Specifically, Mr. Stice was an engineer whereas the new CEO is coming from a more finance angle.

Acquisition

The latest Midland Basin acreage acquisition is the Double Eagle acquisition.

As part of this agreement, there is also some development of less optimal acreage. The actual purchase appears to be top-notch acreage that fits very well into the existing Diamondback footprint.

The Midland Basin appears to be a very profitable area that could become more profitable with the recovery of natural gas prices and the expanding ability of North America to export natural gas. Until very recently, prices in this area were comparatively cheap compared to the profit opportunities.

It would appear that Diamondback will soon have to look towards another ignored area for the company to continue its growth record through acquisitions.

Diamondback has long been one of the first companies to recognize a cheap profit opportunity where no one else is looking. But now it would seem plenty are looking at the Midland Basin. That is pushing prices upwards.

Diamondback has long specialized in the Permian Basin. But if it wants to continue to grow, that strategy may have to change in the future. For the time being the company has an excellent acreage position and plenty of possible drilling sites. So, there is no rush.

There might even be a chance to swap some of the small acreage positions to build more contiguous acreage positions as a growth strategy until a longer-term strategy is in place.

In the meantime, the prospects for the next fiscal year look darn good based upon what the company has already accomplished.

2025 Guidance

This is a relatively small acquisition for the company. Therefore, previous 2025 guidance is not going to change that much. However, when fourth quarter earnings are announced next week, management will likely review the statements already given in light of the latest acquisition.

Given this company's history of acquisitions, investors can likely expect more acquisitions and noncore sales throughout the coming fiscal year.

As with the other acquisitions, management wants to sell some noncore acreage to immediately decrease the debt. The long-term debt level goal would appear to be in the $8 billion to $10 billion range. But that could be revised as the future unfolds.

There may also be another dropdown to Viper Energy Partners (VNOM) of some overriding royalty interests.

Record Of Achievement

There has long been a focus by management on cash flow followed by getting that cash flow back to shareholders through share buybacks and dividends.

Even given the fact that most companies go public on very favorable terms to existing shareholders, the record shown above is going to give the public shareholders some decent long-term returns.

This is one of the very few growth stories that exist in the upstream industry. Now that the company is larger, average growth will slow from the past. However, growth per share in the teens from a combination of organic growth and acquisitions should be a reasonable expectation. Dividend growth and share buybacks will add to the total return to shareholders.

Summary

Returns to shareholders should continue to grow at a good clip even though the company is much larger than when it went public.

The new CEO has a tougher job in that making significant acquisitions becomes more of a challenge as the company grows. That CEO also needs some time to establish a record that is likewise satisfactory to the market.

The company has really just begun to pay dividends of any kind recently. The growth of the dividends from the established base into the future will likely continue into the teens from a combination of organic and acquisition business growth. This company could continue to be a top performer among its peers for some time to come.

The strong buy consideration is enhanced by the currently low price of the stock. This company usually manages to grow throughout the business cycle. It has seamlessly adjusted to market demands for a return of capital while finding ways to grow. That is not a very common management achievement.

Growth is likely to continue well into the future.

Risks

Whenever the CEO changes in any company, there is a period of elevated risk until that new CEO establishes a satisfactory record. Here the challenge of succeeding a CEO with a darn good track record is a bit more than would usually be the case. But if the succession plan works as planned, the transition should be almost invisible to the market.

Any upstream company is subject to the low visibility and high volatility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn could materially change the outlook of the company.

However, the strong investment grade balance sheet and industry-leading low costs would likely mean that much of the industry would suffer a lot more than this company if something like that happened.

data.title: Yield Hunting Part 11: 7.92% YTM With WhiteHorse And Its Baby Bond WHFCL
data.description:
"A stupid person is someone who has the facts, who has the proper information, and still makes the wrong decision." Very well said, in our opinion. In the world of investing, having the right information is essential, but how we use this information depends entirely on our perception. The whole idea of the series is to present to the public information that the average person does not see for one reason or another. In this article, we will focus on another BDC company- WhiteHorse Finance, Inc. (NASDAQ:WHF), along with its baby bond, WHFCL, and see where it stands in the BDC sector. But before we delve deeper into the company's financial metrics, we would like to familiarize you again with the current macroeconomic situation.

Treasury yield curve

The curve has finally started to look like it should after a long period of being inverted. Of course, if you look at it closer, you can still perceive it as flat.

Investment-grade historical spreads

You can see in the chart above the narrow investment-grade spreads in comparison with the 5-year treasury rate.

High-yield historical spreads

The situation in this sector isn't very different. You can see that the high-yield sector's spread is relatively narrow and that it is very close to its historical lows.

SPY Earnings Yield vs. 10-year treasury yield

Bond treasury yields and equity yields are almost inverted right now. This is based on positive market expectations for the earnings growth ratio for the companies in the index. If you opt for the classic portfolio diversification formula, you may end up buying most asset classes at record-high valuations. This is why we monitor any single deviation in fixed-income products, and we want every single product in our portfolio to have "Alpha." Let's now turn our attention to today's BDC.

WHF Overview

WhiteHorse Finance, Inc. is an externally managed, non-diversified, closed-end management investment company that has elected to be treated as a business development company under the Investment Company Act of 1940. Our stock is publicly traded on the NASDAQ exchange under the ticker symbol "WHF.“ Our investment objective is to generate attractive risk-adjusted returns in all market conditions by originating senior secured loans to performing lower middle market companies across a broad range of industries. We believe that market inefficiencies and an imbalance between the supply of, and demand for, capital in the middle market creates attractive investment opportunities. We are defined by a rigorous, disciplined approach to underwriting; a conservative but proactive approach to portfolio growth; and a track record of optimizing partnerships with our borrowers for the benefit of our shareholders.

WHF has a market cap of $271.9 mln and manages a portfolio with a fair value of approximately $654.3 mln distributed in 129 different investments. The company has a total leverage of 51.61% and an expense ratio of 17.86%. The Gross Asset Non-Lev Exp Ratio is 3.96%. WHF's average yield on debt investments is 13.10%.

Now that we've familiarized ourselves with the company's metrics, let's turn our attention to its place in the BDC sector.

BDC bonds yield curve

The charts that we provide aim to give you a general idea of the sector. You can see in the picture below that WHFCL comes up at the top when compared to the best in the sector. This is a clear sign that it does not belong in this group:

Below you can see how WHFCL compares with the lower-quality BDCs:

You can see in the chart below all exchange-tradeable BDCs that are priced above par with yields to call above 5%. WHFCL has a call date of 0.59 years and a YTC of 7.08%. Its YTM is 7.92%.

In the chart below you can see bonds that are trading below par compared to WHFCL:

All of WHFCL's basic information can be seen in the screenshot below:

At the time of writing, WHFCL trades at $25.47, that is, above par value. And since the call date of this bond is approaching quickly, it is vital to know the yield if the company decides to repurchase WHFCL in the future. This is what the YTC curve looks like right now:

Credit Score

Let's now drive our attention to one of the most important aspects of a BDC company- its credit risk. For this, we will use an adjusted version of Moody's approach to assessing financial strength within the Business Development Company (BDC) sector. Moody's considers six key factors when evaluating these companies.

In the chart below, we can look at all the BDC companies and their credit scores based on our adjusted methodology:

WHF is located in the middle, let's see how we got to that conclusion.

Profitability

It is important to note that we do not entirely follow Moody's methodology. Instead of taking the net income, we use the 3-year return on NAV, annualized it, and then divide it by the company's average managed assets. We end up with a score of 7 here or an A3 equivalent.

ACR cushion

This parameter has the highest weight when determining the overall credit score. The regulatory limit for the BDC sector is 150%. The higher- the better. And since WHF's ACR cushion is approximately 29.17%, we get a score of 6 or an A2 equivalent.

Asset quality

WHF's problem loans comprise 3.40% of the portfolio which isn't very low in comparison to its peers. Here, we get a score of 12 or a Ba2.

Senior secured loans represent roughly 78.30% of the portfolio which gives us a score of 9, or a Baa2.

Debt structure

We use this metric to express how WHF's financial managers spread the company's debt throughout the years:

The company has been around since 2011, and we expect proper debt distribution. We can see in the charts above that WHF's financial managers do not disappoint but they still cannot beat ARCC. In the chart below, you can see how WHF compares to the benchmark. WHF receives a score of 9 here or Baa2.

WHF's secured debt makes up 23.64% of the portfolio. Moody's rates such a ratio with a score of 9 which is a Baa2 equivalent. The company's overall credit score is 8.05, or a Baa1. We also adjust the credit score by considering factors such as market capitalization, premium to NAV, historical yield spreads, and number of holdings in the portfolio. After assessing all of these elements, WHF has a final score of 9.50, which is equivalent to a Baa2.

Sector comparison

Below, you can see the bond spread percentile for the whole sector. All of these companies have been rated based on the metrics we used in this article.

We use the best bonds of each company in the sector. In the chart above, you can see how each company's spread percentile rank compares to its credit rating percentile rank. If the blue line is higher than the red one, we are earning more than what we are risking. The opposite is also true: if the red line is higher than the blue line, we are risking too much for what we are earning. In our case, WHFCL sits in a good spot among its peers, and we can see that its credit score and yield are relatively close. There is no significant alpha in WHFCL, and we view it as a normally priced bond. It is added with a lower weight in the portfolio, and we are monitoring for reaching par.

Confession

You might have noticed that our previous articles were mostly about low-duration picks, however, our ultimate goal is to create a portfolio that delivers better earnings than what a BDC company could give you. BDCs typically use leverage and therefore get higher earnings, but as of now, we believe that the market sentiment for these companies is overly optimistic. We want to invest in the debt of these firms, not their equity, as it bears lower credit risk, and by doing so, we will also avoid paying for the absurd and even unnecessary, in our opinion, operating costs that these companies have. Stay tuned for our next article as we will provide a further breakdown of what we are trying to say here.

Conclusion

For the past few weeks, we've been searching for low-duration products that offer high yields and decent credit quality, and WHFCL fits all the criteria. At the time of writing, we will be more than happy if WHFCL is redeemed, and we end up earning a 7.08% yield to call, but our main idea is to create a good substitute portfolio of a BDC company without having to pay for their management.

data.title: SMIN: Keep The Faith In India's Small Caps
data.description:
Commentary on India has turned quite sharply since the selloff began last year. Having been hailed as “the world's best growth story” through the bull phase, media headlines are now focused on whether the Indian economy is “losing steam”.

Sure, there are some genuine threats to the growth story here, including US tariff uncertainties. But for the most part, this has been a cyclical selloff driven by mass foreign investor selling, consumer inflation spikes, as well as a slowdown in quarterly GDP growth. Small- and mid-caps, which are especially levered to economic growth, have begun to underperform in this backdrop - year-to-date, iShares’ MSCI India Small-Cap ETF (BATS:SMIN) is already down by 14.7%.

Still, it’s worth zooming out—over the past five years, India has been one of the world’s best-performing markets, led by its small and mid-cap indices, in turn, well-supported by a rapidly growing earnings base (see SMIN: No Stopping The Boom In India's Smaller Caps).

Macro-wise, the inflation overhang is also clearing, while fiscal and, to a greater extent, monetary policies, are turning expansionary. Currency is a risk, particularly with the current RBI regime focused on growth rather than rupee stability. But it does also help India’s exporters, particularly the smaller cap names, as they tap into 'China Plus One' supply chain shifts. Combined with a step up in policy easing, the higher 'beta' SMIN stands out as a great vehicle to ride a recovery.

SMIN Overview – Smaller but as Competitive as Ever

The iShares MSCI India Small-Cap ETF, which tracks the capitalization-weighted MSCI India Small Cap Index, remains the cleanest vehicle for exposure to India’s smaller caps. For reference, there are India ETFs like the WisdomTree India Earnings Fund (EPI) and VanEck’s India Growth Leaders ETF (GLIN) that also have some small/mid-cap exposure, though these are ‘all-cap’ strategies (i.e., a mix of large/mid/small caps) rather than pure plays.

While SMIN lacks a ‘straight’ comparable, it does charge a very competitive 0.75% expense ratio - a discount to both all-cap factor ETFs (0.87% for EPI and 1.09% gross for GLIN), as well as to iShares’ large-cap-focused India 50 ETF (0.89%)). This is despite primarily investing in a far less liquid Indian small/mid-cap universe.

The other notable here is SMIN’s significantly smaller asset base at $640m (down from $955m when I last covered the fund). Less scale typically means less liquidity and more expensive execution but to iShares’ credit, bid/ask spreads remain very competitive at 0.07%. In sum, on fee structure and execution, SMIN continues to tick all the right boxes.

SMIN Portfolio – Well-Diversified with Lots of Breadth

As for SMIN’s 523-stock portfolio, it is even more spread out than before. The sector composition is mostly intact, led by Industrials, Financials, and Materials. The % allocations, on the other hand, have changed, with Industrials now a much smaller exposure at 20.3%. While Financials (16.9%) and Materials (14.5%) are unchanged, Consumer Discretionary, previously another big sector allocation, has been cut to 12.4%.

Though on a cumulative basis, the top five sectors still account for an outsized 77.2% of the SMIN portfolio, the single-stock breakdown highlights the fund’s actual diversification. A key reason for this is the fund’s sizing policy, which keeps individual positions capped at <2% - note that the current largest holding, IT services firm Coforge, is a ~1.7% allocation. As a result, SMIN tends to stay quite diversified through the cycles, even though individual positions can get reshuffled quite a bit at every rebalance.

SMIN Performance – Still a Serial Outperformer with Lots of ‘Beta’

Having gained strongly through the first half of last year, SMIN sold off along with the rest of the Indian market into year-end. Still, the fund finished 2024 up +17.3% in NAV terms – well ahead of large-cap proxy INDY (+4.0%), as well as ‘all-cap’ ETFs like EPI (+11.1%) and GLIN (+16.1%).

2025, on the other hand, is off to a rocky start. Performance-wise, SMIN has, much like the small-cap universe it tracks, underperformed the India ETF universe, highlighting its high sensitivity (or ‘beta’) to broader market movements.

While this has reduced SMIN’s margin of outperformance over the other Indian tracker funds, it’s worth zooming out here. After all, this is a fund that has compounded capital at a very solid +7.9% and +15.2% pace, respectively, over the last three and five years.

The other silver lining is that SMIN’s persistently wide tracking error (i.e., the difference between fund and benchmark performance), largely due to capital taxes, reverses during bear markets due to the tax offset. Case in point – while the MSCI India Small Cap Index benchmark has lost -13.3% over the last six months, SMIN has drawn down by a much smaller -10.7%.

Other points to note about SMIN’s performance include that it is solely dependent on capital appreciation, as reflected in its near-zero trailing distribution yield. Given that many of SMIN’s portfolio constituents are earlier-stage companies with long earnings growth runways ahead of them, this won’t change anytime soon. So while SMIN won’t suit income investors, there’s plenty here for the growth-oriented, particularly with consensus still calling for a lot more small/mid-cap earnings outgrowth from here.

The fund also remains the most levered to India’s structural asset allocation shift from property and gold into equities (mainly via monthly 401-k-like ‘SIP’ contributions). As for the near term, SMIN offers the highest ‘beta’ to a monetary easing cycle that looks poised to surprise positively now that inflation has receded to a five-month low.

Summing Up

Through all the negative news flow in recent months, it’s worth keeping in mind that India’s small and mid-caps have been among the world’s best performers for a reason. In the meantime, SMIN, having led the way down through India’s latest cyclical downturn (despite still benefiting from some very powerful near and long-term themes), offers a compelling entry point ahead of a cyclical upturn.

data.title: Baker Hughes: Energy Services Firm Will Continue Firing On All Cylinders
data.description:
Intro: A Dividend-Income Energy Equipment And Services Firm

While the energy sector is often covered in terms of big oil companies and producers such as Exxon (XOM) or BP (BP), one firm making the news this week is one that helps make a lot of the drilling even possible, Baker Hughes (NASDAQ:BKR), and that company will be the focus of my article today.

The Houston Texas-based firm was mentioned a few days ago in Reuters, which said that "U.S. energy firms this week added oil and natural gas rigs for a third week in a row for the first time since December 2023."

What this could mean for Baker Hughes is worth keeping an eye on, a firm that calls itself "an energy technology company that has a diverse portfolio of equipment and service capabilities that span the energy and industrial value chain," according to its website.

This stock is also a dividend payer, for my fellow dividend income investors, so today's thesis also explores multiple data points, including key points from its Jan. 30th earnings call, to get a more holistic view of this firm.

Thesis: An Expensive But Valuable Company To Consider In A Dividend Portfolio

My thesis today argues that although this stock trades near 5-year highs and well above its 200-day moving average, there are still plenty of factors to drive further upside potential.

Those include expected demand for natural gas and LNG production, a robust portfolio of solutions across several geographic regions, a very low debt-to-equity along with declining corporate debt, being a proven dividend grower, and having penetrated the potentially lucrative hydrogen energy market... as the shift towards alternative (non-oil) energy is projected to continue.

The following sections discuss these factors in greater depth, and presents why I think this is an expensive but valuable company to have in one's dividend income portfolio.

Macro Picture: Oil Price Outlook Muted In Near Term, But Natural Gas Demand Is Expected

Subscribing to the idea that an experienced investor looks for ideas that show potential to have "future" value, rather than simply considering past results, one factor that I think could impact this entire sector is the macro outlook on oil/gas prices.

Here is the most recent (Feb. 11th) forecast from the US Energy Information Administration:

Gradual increases in production combined with relatively weak global oil demand growth will increase global oil inventories in the second half of 2025 through 2026, placing downward pressure on prices through the remainder of our forecast. As a result, we forecast that the Brent crude oil price will average $74 per barrel in 2025 before falling to $66/b in 2026.

Further, a few weeks prior to that, Citigroup (C) had slightly raised their target avg. oil price for 2025 (forecasting Brent Crude avg. $67/barrel) but also noted that the current White House has "laid out a sweeping plan to maximise oil and gas production, including declaring a national energy emergency to speed up permitting, rolling back environmental protections, and withdrawing the U.S. from the Paris climate pact," according to another Reuters article.

Based on the above forecasts, it keeps avg. oil prices significantly below the highs they saw in early 2022, according to the Statista chart below, where I highlighted in blue lines where the 2025 oil price avg. forecast range lies:

At the same time, it is notable to mention that Baker Hughes in its FY24 Q4 results commentary by the CEO, indicated that natural gas and LNG will provide tailwind to this sector due to rising demand:

We believe that natural gas and LNG demand will demonstrate accelerated growth, which will drive increasing demand for our gas-levered products and solutions... demand for U.S. natural gas is set to increase by approximately 20 bcf per day, or 18% by 2030, led by a continued increase in gas requirements for new LNG facilities and data centers.

So, from the indicators mentioned, I think a combination of the gas demand forecast and the "drill baby drill" approach of the new White House could drive near-term demand for this firm's products/services, while the oil price forecast is not so disastrous to make it too unprofitable to drill for oil.

Top And Bottom-Line Growth Forecasts Positive, Along With Proven Profitability

Besides understanding the macro environment for this sector, it's worth considering company-specific growth expectations.

First, expected fwd revenue growth for Baker Hughes is 4.39%, according to Seeking Alpha data, putting it well ahead of its overall sector.

To get more granular, I also compared growth expectations vs. similar peers and for those I picked Schlumberger (SLB), Halliburton (HAL), and Weatherford (WFRD), all of which are listed as oil/gas equipment and servicing companies.

From the data, we can see that Baker Hughes is pretty competitive vs. this peer group on fwd revenue growth, and beats peers in fwd EBITDA growth and EPS growth, which I believe will drive upside by attracting more future-minded investors to this firm, supporting the share price upward.

In addition, the analyst consensus calls for a +10% YoY EPS growth this year, with 13 upward revisions already, helping drive the bullish case further.

Baker Hughes with its net income margin of nearly 11% also beats its overall sector, which tells me it is more efficient at turning revenue into profits.

On a longer-term trend, in fact, this firm has proven itself as able to grow its adjusted EPS, as its Q4 presentation shows, and while this may be historic data it also points to a management that has achieved results so far, and I think serious investors do care about there being a proven model for profitability growth:

A Diversified Product Portfolio Can Lower Business Risk

In several articles, I have touted the importance of a diversified portfolio to reduce the risk of over-reliance on one single product, solution, or geography, and so along those lines I went looking for diversification at Baker Hughes too.

For example, Q4 results from the firm's industrial and energy technology business segment shows there was YoY growth in orders across a diverse portfolio including gas tech, industrial tech, and climate tech products/solutions, all of which contributing to the overall segment growth in orders:

At the same time, however, from the same presentation we can also see that the oilfield services and equipment business segment had some mixed results in terms of YoY revenue growth, with positive growth achieved only by the subsea systems segment, and in the EU/CIS/Africa geography, while other segments and geographies saw YoY declines:

I believe such a portfolio diversified across many solutions, as well as geographies, could be an upside factor as it lowers business risk but also drives the Baker Hughes brand as one with a portfolio of solutions in this sector, rather than a single innovation.

While some segments may see declines, overall for the firm those declines can be muted or dampened by the segments that have achieved growth.

A Low Debt/Equity Ratio Vs. Key Peers, And Declining Long-Term Debt

When it comes to debt risk, a notable point about this firm I would highlight is that its debt/equity ratio is only 0.35, and it has kept a relatively steady D/E for the last 3 years, remaining below 3 of its key peers:

Coupled with that, I would point out that comparing the company's long-term debt in Dec 2020 vs. Dec 2024, they were able to reduce it by 11%, which, I think, is a worthy achievement.

Now, some readers may say I am overly debt-averse and that debt is necessary to grow the business in some cases, however I would argue that in an elevated interest-rate environment it makes more sense to do the opposite... reduce debt... and wait for lower interest-rate environments to finance business growth.

Thus, I consider this type of debt profile an upside factor. As the firm is not overly leveraged now, eventually when a low-interest environment returns it will be in a better position to grow through cheaper financing if needed.

A Low-Yield Dividend Stock, With Proven Dividend Growth Factors

As a dividend income stock, and one of my dividend ideas for the week, what should be said about the dividend case is that it is not one of high yield, with a div yield around 1.9% even though the sector average is closer to 4%.

It makes a stronger case in terms of proven 5 year dividend growth. For example, it had a positive 5 year div growth CAGR of 3.6%, although it came up slightly behind the sector average.

It also beat its 3 key peers I mentioned earlier, in terms of proven 5 yr div growth CAGR, which is impressive:

Looking ahead to further dividend sustainability and growth potential, which I think matters to dividend investors, I am confident about the direction because this firm is a strong cashflow generator. For example, its projected fwd operating cashflow growth is 8.8%, much higher than its sector average.

Its free cashflow/FCF per share is also expected to grow nearly 11%, while the sector avg. is closer to a mere 1%.

From another angle, we can see from cashflow data that the firm has grown free cashflow/share for 2 quarters in a row so far:

So, while this may not be a stock with a 10% dividend yield, the upside case here is the growth and stability of the dividend, since that can contribute to future dividend income of shareholders.

Share Price Trading Near Highs And Considerably Overvalued to Peers

I wanted to leave a discussion of price and valuation until after going over all those other factors, in order to help understand what could be driving the price and valuation.

Since this is my first time covering this stock, I don't have a prior rating of mine to compare against, however take a look at the chart I created below which compares the most recent share price vs. the 5 year high/low and the 200-day simple moving average:

As we can see, this stock is now +24% above its 200-day SMA, and nearing its 5-year-high.

So, I think likely the market has already priced in all of the positive upside factors I mentioned, since a lot of those metrics are publicly available since the last earnings call, and the bulls have come out after this stock in full force.

Coupled with valuation data, this stock is trading at a fwd earnings multiple of 18x, when comparing its fwd P/E (GAAP) vs. the sector which is closer to a 12x multiple.

On a more granular level, compared to its key peers in oil/gas servicing, it is the most overvalued:

So, despite all the positive factors I described so far in this thesis, an investor should consider whether all of that has made this stock just too expensive to buy right now.

In the next section, I will point out a potential downside risk to consider which may or may not slow the pace of all these bulls.

Additional Risk To Consider: Growth Of Renewable Energy Future Demand

Because Baker Hughes may be known primarily a company servicing the oil/gas/LNG space, naturally its business in a longer-term sense could depend heavily on continued growth of demand from those energy sources.

Some data suggests that the future demand growth will actually come from other energy sources.

For example, Statista data suggests that by 2050 it will actually be renewable energy sources that have a greater growth in consumption, as their chart below suggests.

Such a future outlook could be at least somewhat of a longer-term downside factor not only for Baker Hughes but its entire sector, in terms of those business units producing products and solutions for the major oil producers.

However, on a closer look at what it is that Baker Hughes actually does, and this also points to the importance of a diversified portfolio, we can see that it has already made strides in being a player in the alternative energy market and not simply the oil sector.

One of those energy sources is hydrogen.

Baker Hughes says the following about its potential:

Hydrogen (H2) is uniquely positioned among energy carriers to serve a range of functions in a low-carbon economy with important applications in heating, mobility, power generation, and especially to reduce emissions from hard-to-abate sectors.

In fact, the firm has already had experience in that space, since in 2009 it "built the first turbine in the world to run on 100% hydrogen for the Fusina Hydrogen Power Project in Italy."

Further, the company's case study said "the innovative plant demonstrated industrial-scale electricity production in an H2 fueled plant."

So, although a greater focus in the future may be renewable energy sources and less on oil, at the same time it seems Baker Hughes is already trying to get ahead of the game by expanding its portfolio beyond just the oil segment.

Conclusion: Bullish Sentiment Agrees With Wall Street

In conclusion, although part of me would love to say hold or sell at such a high share price and valuation, the holistic view based on my rating sheet below clearly points to a buy, as the upside factors are much stronger than the downside ones:

Compared to the consensus shown on the stock page today, my buy rating agrees with the Wall St. consensus:

Wrap Up: An Interesting Year for Energy

As this article is part of my ongoing theme on this platform for 2025 called Financial Markets with Albert Anthony, where I focus more on growing a dividend income portfolio, I welcome readers' comments below on where you see the energy sector going in 2025, amidst the constant headlines of geopolitical conflicts, and how you think this could impact a stock like this, beyond the thesis I presented.

data.title: Equity Markets: Nearing A Peak Or More Room To Run?
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist and Han Peng, CFA, Director, Multi-Asset Research

While investors are increasingly concerned about whether markets have peaked, bull markets don’t just die of old age. History suggests that the Fed plays an outsized role in determining market returns based on its monetary policy stance.

While today’s macro environment doesn’t resemble previous market peaks, and there remains a path for further market upside, policy uncertainty poses a significant challenge to sustaining bullish sentiment.

Following double-digit returns for U.S. equities over the past two years, and with valuations now incredibly expensive, investors are increasingly concerned about whether markets have peaked.

Bull markets don’t just die of old age, however, and history suggests that the Federal Reserve tends to play an outsized role in determining the prevailing market regime based on its monetary policy stance.

Indeed, most market sell-offs larger than 10% since 1965 were triggered by either the Fed pivoting aggressively to a hawkish stance and spooking markets or the Fed staying restrictive for too long, putting downward pressure on growth and earnings.

Today’s macroeconomic landscape differs meaningfully from past market peaks, particularly as economic data show little sign of a hard landing while labor market conditions remain resilient. A narrow but viable path remains for markets to grind higher, especially if earnings growth continues to deliver as expected.

However, uncertainty around future policy decisions poses a significant risk. Inflation remains a key concern, and the new Trump administration’s fast-moving policy agenda could potentially impact the labor market and further complicate the inflationary picture.

These will add challenges to Fed policymaking, and although the bar for a rate hike remains high, a hawkish pivot could bring an abrupt end to the current bull market.

Prudent investors should remain vigilant, balancing optimism with a strategic approach to risk in today’s evolving market environment.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Topgolf Callaway: Q4 Preview, A Costly Mistake With No Clear Turnaround - 'Sell'
data.description:
The merger of Topgolf Callaway Brands Corp. (NYSE:MODG) was completed during the pandemic golf boom. Following the acquisition, Topgolf contributed approximately 40% of the overall revenue of the newly formed company and experienced significant growth in FY22. However, as economic conditions normalized in FY23, with tightening consumer spending and rising interest rates, Topgolf Callaway has experienced a revenue slowdown, and then declining into FY24. Currently, with a $1.3 billion market cap and total debt reaching $4.5 billion, I do not foresee a turnaround for Topgolf Callaway, even the intended spin-off isn’t likely to secure a safe landing. Thus, I initiate a “SELL” to Topgolf Callaway, and estimate the fair value at the moment is $2.

The Merger Was a Mistake

I do think the merger of Topgolf and Callaway is a mistake, for several reasons:

  1. Strong profitability relies on dedicated golfers, not recreational golfers or budget-conscious consumers. In my earlier article about Acushnet Holdings (GOLF), the company that owns premium brands like Titleist, FootJoy, Scotty Cameron, etc., I shared my view that dedicated golfers are the ones who have passion and inner drive to commit the time, effort and money in search of better golfing performance. They are usually financially affluent and less sensitive to economic slowdown, and they are willing to purchase new products and premium products, which secure strong profitability in the long run. Topgolf, on the contrary, is a social place and mainly caters to casual and recreational golfers. These spendings are not sticky or recurring in nature, many of those are just one-off revenue opportunities. The cross-sell and upsell into Callaway premium product opportunities are also very limited.
  2. The two business models are very different. Topgolf is highly CapEx-intensive, it requires heavy cashflow for new venue openings, leases, decorations, and constructions. This model rapidly increased the debt level for the combined company, especially in a rising interest rate environment. The high correlation between the two businesses makes the growth slowdown even worse. While consumers are spending less on golf balls and gears, recreational golfers visit Topgolf venues less frequently, and corporations are more sensitive to the economic slowdown and reduced their budgets and events at Topgolf. Serious golfers, when they have to practice with indoor golf simulators, would mostly likely go to private simulator bays instead of paying a premium to Topgolf, which is mostly designed for fun.
  3. It damages the Callaway brand image. Callaway was historically positioned as a premium golf brand competing with Titleist and other high-end names. It had significant exposures in global tours, with top professionals endorsing its equipment and apparel. Topgolf, is more of an entertainment brand, mainly catering to casual and recreational golfers. As a passionate golfer myself, I personally stopped purchasing Callaway products knowing Topgolf is burning cash and causing operational disruption in the back end. I started to question their product quality, whether Callaway still has enough effort and financial resources to even invest in product R&D and study the market trends, and I question whether Callaway is still a high-end brand anymore.

Recent Results

On November 12th, 2024 Topgolf Callaway released its Q3-FY24 earnings. Very disappointing results, with a decline in revenue, net income and EPS.

Looking at each product line, year-to-date revenue growth of golf equipment is down 2.6% compared to the same period of FY23, active lifestyle is much worse with a decrease of 10.3%. The FY24 golf participation and engagement rate is reported to be slightly up by 2% vs FY23, so the decrease in the Callaway golf segment is very concerning. I think it is a sign of Callaway losing market share and customer trust.

The only increase came from Topgolf segment revenue. However, same venue sales through Q1 to Q3 all decreased significantly compared to the prior year. Management shared in the Q3-FY24 earnings call that a decrease in same venue sales, especially larger bays, and a decrease in average customer spend each contributed to about half of the total decrease. The 3.7% total revenue increase purely came from an increased number of venues. Again, a large amount of CapEx is required to open up new venues, which adds up to the current debt level and burns cash.

Q4-FY24 Preview: More Downside Risks

Management updated their full year 2024 outlook in the Q3-FY24 earnings call, and I took it as a downgrade. It looks like that Topgolf revenue guidance is not changed, but overall revenue and EBITDA guidance are expected to be even lower than the last guidance, meaning Callaway golf equipment and apparels continue to decrease.

Topgolf Callaway is about to release Q4-FY24 result on February 25th, 2025. Based on management's last guidance, the below charts show how it looks like on segment revenue by quarter, and quarter-over-quarter growth rate. With Topgolf revenue barely flat, Lifestyle continues to decrease from prior year, it means an unrealistic 20% growth in golf equipment is required to meet management's Q4 guidance. With Q4 being a slow equipment quarter historically, I highly doubt if Topgolf Callaway would successfully deliver.

Outlook and Valuation

Looking forward, I do think each of Topgolf Callaway’s business segments will grow at their normal rates. I expect 5% yoy growth on Callaway golf equipment and apparel from both price and volume increase, while 7% yoy growth on Topgolf venues from new venue openings, and increased per customer spend and same venue sames. Together, it means 5.9% CAGR for the next decade.

On profitability, I expect slight cost improvement and margin expansion. I expect 20 basis points yoy cost improvement and 20 basis points yoy improvement on G&A expenses due to innovation and operational efficiencies. I expect its R&D spend will continue to grow with revenue growth rate, to ensure enough investment to keep its products as innovative as its competitors.

The WACC is calculated to be 11.0% assuming: risk-free rate 3.8%; beta 1.14; equity risk premium 7%; cost of debt 12.7%; equity $1.34 billion; debt $4.45 billion; tax rate 15%. The DCF is summarized as follows:

Discounting all the future FCF, the fair value is calculated to be $2 per share, as per my estimates.

Spin-off Considerations

On September 4th, 2024, Topgolf Callaway announced an intended spin-off between Topgolf and Callaway targeting mid FY25. However, there has been no further update since this announcement.

The implication is not reflected in my model above. If the spin-off proceeds, Callaway will assume all current long-term debt, significantly changing its prior risk profile. My preliminary estimation of Callaway’s standalone fair value post spin-off is $20 per share. However, there remain too many uncertainties around this potential transaction – it could be delayed, it could take longer than expected to execute, the legal fee bill could be a lot higher, daily operations could be disrupted, and corporate morale could be very negatively impacted. I would not go bullish until the dust settled.

Potential Upside Risks

  • Earning Surprise. Q4 earnings may beat management guidance and lift the stock price upwards. However, I think it’s only temporary. With the structural challenges of Topgolf, it will be tough for Topgolf Callaway to properly manage liquidity and leverage level.
  • Positive Market Response to Spin-off News. Investors may regain confidence if Topgolf Callaway management announces the decision of spin-off. I think this can only be temporary, and long-term financial challenges will persist.

Conclusion

To me, the merger of Topgolf and Callaway was a strategic failure. The expected synergies never materialized, instead Callaway’s premium brand image suffered, debt levels skyrocketed, and financial management became increasingly difficult in the last few quarters. I do not see a turnaround for Topgolf Callaway together as a combined company, and even the planned spin-off carries significant uncertainties. Thus, I initiate a 'Sell' to Topgolf Callaway.

data.title: Weak PMIs Validate Our Recession Risk Call
data.description:
On Friday, the Services PMI for January dropped to 49.7 which indicates that services are now contracting. In addition, existing homes sales declined 5% M/M and consumer sentiment came in at 64.7 vs. expectations of 67.8.

January retail sales declined a disconcerting .8% last week and the Q4 GDP report weakened to 2.3% from over 3% the prior quarter, with the critical investment component dropping 1.1%.

The old economy is entering into a recession with both commercial and residential investment contracting as is normal during a Fed tightening cycle. Very strong tech investment is an offset that could keep the US out of a near term recession until the Fed acts.

Weakness in the old economy has been driven by the sell-off in the 10-year treasury to a 4.8% in response to harsh Fed rhetoric, causing the 30-year mortgage rate to rise above 7%.

We continue to believe that the Fed’s policy framework is very dangerous, as they use a price index that has a two-year lag and pursue a 2% target that is too low and led to the Great Financial Crisis.

Real-time CPI, using market prices for rents, is already tracking below the Fed’s 2% target at 1.8%. Inflation is exclusively caused by excessive monetary growth, but the money supply (M0) has shrunk 5% Y/Y indicating we may enter a deflation if the Fed does not act. If the Fed does not cut rates in 2025, the US almost certainly will enter recession in 2026.
